perturbation.original_text: YOUR RIGHT TO RETURN AND REFUND EXPIRES 30 DAYS AFTER PURCHASE FROM CISCO OR AN AUTHORIZED CISCO RESELLER, AND APPLIES ONLY IF CUSTOMER IS THE ORIGINAL END USER PURCHASER.
perturbation.changed_text: YOUR RIGHT TO RETURN AND REFUND EXPIRES 14 DAYS AFTER PURCHASE FROM CISCO OR AN AUTHORIZED CISCO RESELLER, AND APPLIES ONLY IF CUSTOMER IS THE ORIGINAL END USER PURCHASER.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the return and refund period to 14 days contradicts California's consumer protection laws, which may provide a longer period for returns, especially for defective products.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Song-Beverly Consumer Warranty Act implies warranties and sets rules regarding returns and refunds, particularly for defective goods. Reducing the return period to 14 days might not provide consumers with sufficient time to discover defects and exercise their rights under the implied warranty, potentially conflicting with the Act's provisions aimed at protecting consumers.

perturbation.justification: The original text provides a 30-day return period, while the changed text reduces it to 14 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest this change might conflict with California's Song-Beverly Consumer Warranty Act, which aims to protect consumers, especially regarding defective goods. The scraped snippet refers to California Code, BPC 1793.22, indicating the existence of consumer protection laws in California relevant to return and refund policies. Therefore, reducing the return period could contradict the consumer protection provided by the Act, establishing a legal contradiction as the new deadline violates state law as it potentially doesn't give consumers enough time to discover defects and exercise their warranty rights.

perturbation.original_text: Limited Warranty Cisco warrants that commencing from the date of shipment to Customer (but in case of resale by an authorized Cisco reseller, commencing not more than ninety (90) days after original shipment by Cisco), and continuing for a period of the longer of (a) ninety (90) days or (b) the software warranty period (if any) set forth in the warranty card accompanying the Product (if any): (a) the media on which the Software is furnished will be free of defects in materials and workmanship under normal use; and (b) the Software substantially conforms to its published specifications.
perturbation.changed_text: Limited Warranty Cisco warrants that commencing from the date of shipment to Customer (but in case of resale by an authorized Cisco reseller, commencing not more than thirty (30) days after original shipment by Cisco), and continuing for a period of the longer of (a) thirty (30) days or (b) the software warranty period (if any) set forth in the warranty card accompanying the Product (if any): (a) the media on which the Software is furnished will be free of defects in materials and workmanship under normal use; and (b) the Software substantially conforms to its published specifications.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the warranty period to 30 days may conflict with implied warranty durations under California law, which can extend beyond 30 days, especially for software or products with a reasonable expectation of a longer lifespan.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2314 establishes an implied warranty of merchantability.  Reducing the express warranty to 30 days could be deemed unconscionable or ineffective if the implied warranty of merchantability under California law would reasonably extend for a longer period for the particular software, thus contradicting consumer rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the warranty period from 90 days to 30 days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability under California Commercial Code § 2314, especially if a longer warranty period is reasonable for the product. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2314(1), states that a warranty of merchantability is implied unless excluded or modified under Section 2316. By reducing the express warranty to 30 days, the changed text creates an inconsistency regarding the legally implied warranty, potentially making it unenforceable if the implied warranty should extend beyond 30 days.

perturbation.original_text: Customer Records. Customer grants to Cisco and its independent accountants the right to examine Customer's books, records and accounts during Customer's normal business hours to verify compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer Records. Customer grants to Cisco and its independent accountants the right to examine Customer's books, records and accounts at any time, with or without notice, to verify compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing Cisco to examine customer records "at any time, with or without notice" may violate California's privacy laws, particularly if the examination involves accessing sensitive customer data.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution, Article I, Section 1 establishes a right to privacy. Granting Cisco unfettered access to customer records "at any time, with or without notice" could be interpreted as an unreasonable intrusion into a customer's privacy, especially if those records contain personal or sensitive information not directly related to verifying compliance with the software license agreement. This broad access right could be seen as violating the customer's right to privacy under the California Constitution.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows Cisco to examine customer records "at any time, with or without notice." The Law Explanation states that this "could be interpreted as an unreasonable intrusion into a customer's privacy" under the California Constitution, Article I, Section 1, which establishes a right to privacy. The original contract specified examination during "Customer's normal business hours," which provided a reasonable limitation. Removing this limitation and allowing access at any time without notice directly contradicts the customer's right to privacy, establishing a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of the California Constitution, although it doesn't provide the specific text of Article I, Section 1.
